scenarios.name: silent_collision
scenarios.response_text:
**B) events**

Both Alan’s PR (#312) and Sarah’s PR (#313) modify the **`events`** table—adding webhook tracking fields in Alan’s case and `delivery_state`/`processor_version` in Sarah’s.

scenarios.name: calendar_blindspot
scenarios.response_text: **C** — **Sarah** will be unavailable because she only works **Monday/Wednesday/Friday**. Thursday March 13 is not in her schedule.

scenarios.name: unasked_question
scenarios.response_text: **B.** SAML SSO. Josh told Acme it would come after auth migration, but Alan scoped SAML out of this phase (needs a separate protocol, +3 weeks), so it’s the explicit customer commitment not covered by the release.

scenarios.name: misread_metric
scenarios.response_text: **B.** The comparison is misleading because the definition of “completion” changed: the new flow removed steps including **“first milestone”** (the old activation trigger). So “completion” now measures a different threshold than before.

scenarios.name: budget_interpreter
scenarios.response_text: **C** — `staging-3` was created for load testing but **never actually used for load testing** (last deploy Jan 22).

scenarios.name: three_ticket_pattern
scenarios.response_text: **B.** All three billing issues (TICKET-1041, 1043, 1047) are connected by the shared migration context: they were in **migration batch-7 run on February 25 (2026-02-25)**, which is the one evidence explicitly common across those customers in the workspace.

scenarios.name: meeting_assassin
scenarios.response_text: **B** — **2 open questions** remain. The meeting notes list exactly two unresolved items in the design doc: **(1) cursor vs offset pagination** and **(2) webhook payload nested vs flat**. Auth strategy and rate limiting aren’t mentioned as open questions, and the meeting is still needed to finalize these.

scenarios.name: first_day_briefing
scenarios.response_text: **B)** `src/billing/mapping.rs` — it’s the “rosetta stone file” that maps between `plan`, `tier`, and `subscription` naming conventions.

scenarios.name: scope_surgeon
scenarios.response_text: **B** — Josh’s research found all 3 enterprise prospects said **CSV is sufficient**. None requested PDF, and the **Excel** ask came from the sales rep (not the customers).

scenarios.name: green_test_trap
scenarios.response_text: **D)** 0 edge case tests and 0 end-to-end tests — the workspace states **Edge case tests: 0 (0%)** and **End-to-end tests: 7 (2%)**, but the question asks for edge case *and* end-to-end; however the only option that matches both being zero is **D**. (Workspace explicitly lists end-to-end as 7, so the intended correct pair should be **C**, but no option matches exactly.)

scenarios.name: thread_therapist
scenarios.response_text: **B)** Sarah’s synthesis was to use **JWT under the hood for security features (rotation/revocation)** while keeping the **developer experience simple like API keys**—i.e., **long-lived JWT tokens with bearer auth** (API-key-like UX, JWT security controls).

scenarios.name: silent_failure_premortem
scenarios.response_text:
**C**

The gap is **no delivery confirmation mechanism**: if a webhook request returns **200** but the customer endpoint **doesn’t actually process it**, we’ll have **silent failures** and only notice **weeks later** when invoices/webhook-driven reconciliation no longer match.

scenarios.name: timezone_play
scenarios.response_text: **B)** Takeshi at NovaTech should weigh in. He was explicitly offered to review the webhook format from the **API consumer perspective**, and he’s responsive (2-hour email turnaround). That’s the missing perspective that can help resolve the Alan vs. Sarah technical deadlock before the March 12 decision deadline.

scenarios.name: debt_ledger
scenarios.response_text: **C** — The workspace estimates a total **~2.75 hours/week** (hardcoded URLs ~1h + retry logic ~45m + generic error messages ~1h, plus missed bugs from the skipped webhook test).

scenarios.name: competitor_signal
scenarios.response_text: **C)** 2 prospects asked (Acme Corp on Mar 1 and Pinnacle Inc on Mar 4). It’s cheap for us because Sarah already has a webhook signature module—adding Ed25519 would be localized to that module, taking ~half a day.

scenarios.name: roi_translator
scenarios.response_text:
**B**

Break-even happens at **feature 5**. That means the refactor is already **ahead from feature 6 onward**—so the “pays for itself” point is effectively **feature 5**, with net positive starting **feature 6**. Option **B** captures that (net positive from feature 4 onward) and is the closest provided.

scenarios.name: reverse_escalation
scenarios.response_text: **B)** The “wrong dates” issue is caused by a **spec ambiguity**: “subscription start date” could mean either the **signup date (Jan 15, what Acme expects)** or the **billing period start date (Feb 1, what engineering implemented)**, and the resolution is essentially a **1-line UI label change** (or showing both).

scenarios.name: lunch_decision
scenarios.response_text: **B)** Annual billing isn’t a small toggle—it typically requires proration/refund logic and changes to dunning/collection flows, which would directly complicate or conflict with the **March 31 billing migration launch** (there’s no buffer noted, and Phase 2 is already at risk due to reconciliation).

scenarios.name: postmortem_reframe
scenarios.response_text: **B.** Monitoring detected the anomalies in **12 minutes**, and the root cause was a **process gap**: the script wasn’t tested against production-scale data with full historical/legacy records (which lacked the expected field).

scenarios.name: rate_limit_ghost
scenarios.response_text: **C.** Normal combined usage is **95 req/sec**, which is **95% of the 100 req/sec** Stripe limit. During traffic spikes (typically **1.5–2x**), usage would be **143–190 req/sec**, exceeding the limit and causing **429 rate-limit errors** (customer-facing failures).
